content:
# Genesis Chapter 43 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25v–26r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_043_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_043_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 43–44

---

*The Famine Presses — Jacob Reluctant to Send Benjamin (vv. 1–5)*

**43:1** And the famine was severe in the land.

**43:2** And it came to pass when they had finished eating the grain that they had brought from Egypt, that their father said to them, "Go back, buy us a little food."

**43:3** And Judah said to him, saying, "The man solemnly warned us, saying, 'You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.'

**43:4** If you will send our brother with us, we will go down and buy food for you.

**43:5** But if you will not send him, we will not go down, for the man said to us, 'You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.'"

---

*Judah's Surety Speech — Jacob Relents (vv. 6–14)*

**43:6** And Israel said, "Why did you do evil to me, to tell the man that you had another brother?"

**43:7** And they said, "The man asked carefully about us and about our kindred, saying, 'Is your father still alive? Do you have another brother?' And we told him according to these words. Could we know at all that he would say, 'Bring your brother down'?"

**43:8** And Judah said to Israel his father, "Send the boy with me, and we will arise and go, that we may live and not die — both we and you and also our little ones.

**43:9** I myself will be surety for him — from my hand you may require him. If I do not bring him back to you and set him before you, then I will have sinned against you all my days.

**43:10** For if we had not delayed, surely by now we would have returned twice."

**43:11** And Israel their father said to them, "If it must be so, then do this: take of the choicest produce of the land in your vessels and bring down to the man a gift — a little balm and a little honey, tragacanth and laudanum, pistachios and almonds.

**43:12** And take double the silver in your hand, and the silver that was returned in the mouth of your sacks, bring back in your hand — perhaps it was an oversight.

**43:13** Take also your brother, and arise, go back to the man.

**43:14** And may El Shaddai give you compassion before the man, that he may release to you your other brother and Benjamin. And as for me — as I am bereaved, I am bereaved."

---

*The Brothers Arrive in Egypt with Benjamin — Joseph Commands the Feast (vv. 15–17)*

**43:15** And the men took this gift, and they took double the silver in their hand, and Benjamin. And they arose and went down to Egypt and stood before Joseph.

**43:16** And Joseph saw Benjamin with them, and he said to the one who was over his house, "Bring the men into the house, and slaughter an animal and prepare it, for the men will eat with me at noon."

**43:17** And the man did as Joseph said, and the man brought the men to Joseph's house.

---

*The Brothers Fear at Joseph's House — The Steward Reassures Them (vv. 18–23)*

**43:18** And the men were afraid because they were brought to Joseph's house, and they said, "Because of the silver that was returned in our sacks the first time we are being brought in — to roll over us and fall upon us and take us as slaves, and our donkeys."

**43:19** And they approached the man who was over Joseph's house and spoke to him at the entrance of the house.

**43:20** And they said, "Please, my lord — we came down the first time to buy food.

**43:21** And it came to pass when we came to the lodging place and we opened our sacks, and behold — each man's silver was in the mouth of his sack, our silver in its full weight. And we have brought it back in our hand.

**43:22** And other silver we have brought down in our hand to buy food. We do not know who put our silver in our sacks."

**43:23** And he said, "Peace to you — do not be afraid. Your God and the God of your father has given you treasure in your sacks. Your silver came to me." And he brought Simeon out to them.

---

*Reception at Joseph's House — The Gifts Presented (vv. 24–26)*

**43:24** And the man brought the men into Joseph's house, and gave them water and they washed their feet, and he gave fodder to their donkeys.

**43:25** And they prepared the gift for the coming of Joseph at noon, for they had heard that they would eat bread there.

**43:26** And Joseph came to the house, and they brought him the gift that was in their hand into the house, and they bowed down to him to the ground.

---

*Joseph Inquires About His Father — He Sees Benjamin — He Weeps (vv. 27–31)*

**43:27** And he asked them about their welfare and said, "Is your father well, the old man of whom you spoke? Is he still alive?"

**43:28** And they said, "Your servant our father is well — he is still alive." And they bowed down and prostrated themselves.

**43:29** And he lifted up his eyes and saw Benjamin his brother, the son of his mother, and said, "Is this your youngest brother of whom you spoke to me?" And he said, "God be gracious to you, my son."

**43:30** And Joseph hurried — for his compassion was stirred toward his brother, and he sought to weep. And he went into the inner room and wept there.

**43:31** And he washed his face and came out and controlled himself, and said, "Set out bread."

---

*The Meal — Egyptians Cannot Eat with Hebrews — Benjamin's Five Portions (vv. 32–34)*

**43:32** And they set out for him by himself and for them by themselves and for the Egyptians who ate with him by themselves, for the Egyptians could not eat bread with the Hebrews — for that is an abomination to Egypt.

**43:33** And they sat before him — the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth. And the men looked at one another in astonishment.

**43:34** And he took portions to them from before him, and Benjamin's portion was five times greater than any of their portions. And they drank and were drunk with him.

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "Severe" — kaved (v. 1): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders kaved as "heavy" (the literal meaning of the root k-v-d); LOGOS and PNEUMA render "severe." Both are valid — the root means "heavy, weighty, burdensome." In English, "the famine was heavy" sounds slightly unusual; "severe" is the more natural collocate for famine. The k-v-d root's connection to kavod ("glory, weight") is a rich intertextual thread, but "severe" communicates the sense more effectively in context. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "severe."

**2. "Choicest produce" — zimrat ha'arets (v. 11): EMET upheld**

EMET renders "choicest produce"; LOGOS renders "choicest products"; PNEUMA renders "finest products." The Hebrew zimrah is a rare word — possibly from zamar ("to prune" or "to praise"), yielding "the praised/pruned things." The items listed (balm, honey, tragacanth, laudanum, pistachios, almonds) are all agricultural/natural products. "Produce" carries an appropriate agricultural resonance. "Choicest" preserves more of the Hebrew's evaluative force than "finest." SOPHIA follows EMET: "choicest produce."

**3. "As I am bereaved, I am bereaved" — ka'asher shakolti shakalti (v. 14): EMET upheld**

EMET renders "as I am bereaved, I am bereaved"; LOGOS renders "if I am bereaved, I am bereaved"; PNEUMA renders "if I am to be bereaved, I will be bereaved." The Hebrew particle ka'asher means "as, just as, according as" — not "if" ('im). The LXX adds the conditional εἰ ("if"), and the traditional English reading follows the LXX. But the Hebrew grammar supports a resigned statement of existing fact: "as indeed I have been bereaved, I am bereaved." Jacob is not hypothesizing — he is accepting what has already happened (the loss of Joseph) and what may yet happen. The flat resignation of EMET's rendering is more faithful to the Hebrew syntax and more devastating in tone. SOPHIA follows EMET: "as I am bereaved, I am bereaved."

**4. "Compassion" — rachamim (v. 14): LOGOS upheld**

EMET and PNEUMA render rachamim as "mercy"; LOGOS renders "compassion." Both are standard translations of rachamim (the plural of rechem, "womb"). In this chapter, the word rachamim appears twice: in Jacob's prayer (v. 14) and in the description of Joseph's emotional reaction (v. 30: nikmeru rachamav, "his compassion was stirred"). Using "compassion" in both verses preserves the verbal thread — Jacob prays for compassion; Joseph feels compassion. "Mercy" is more traditional but does not connect the two occurrences as clearly. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "compassion."

**5. "To roll over us and fall upon us" — l'hitgolel aleinu (v. 18): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders "to roll against us and to fall upon us"; LOGOS renders "to roll over us and fall upon us"; PNEUMA renders "set upon us, overwhelm us." The Hebrew l'hitgolel (Hitpael of g-l-l, "to roll") is a vivid physical metaphor — the image of being rolled upon, crushed, overwhelmed by force. PNEUMA's "set upon us, overwhelm us" paraphrases away the distinctive rolling image. EMET and LOGOS both preserve the Hebrew's physical force. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "to roll over us and fall upon us."

**6. "His compassion was stirred" — nikmeru rachamav (v. 30): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders "his compassion burned"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "his compassion was stirred." The Niphal of kamar means "to grow warm, to be kindled, to be deeply stirred" — used in only two other places (1 Kgs 3:26; Hos 11:8), both describing overwhelming involuntary love. "Burned" captures the intensity but sounds unusual in English with "compassion" as subject. "Was stirred" is less intense but more natural and still conveys the involuntary, deep upwelling of emotion. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "his compassion was stirred."

**7. "Controlled himself" — vayyit'appaq (v. 31): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render "controlled himself"; PNEUMA renders "composed himself." The Hithpael of aleph-p-q means "to restrain oneself, to hold oneself back." "Controlled" captures the active restraint — Joseph is forcing his emotion back. "Composed" suggests achieving calm, but the Hebrew emphasizes the effort of holding in. The word recurs in 45:1 where Joseph "could not control himself" (lo yakol l'hit'appeq) — the release of what is here held back. Using "controlled" preserves the verbal thread. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "controlled himself."

**8. "Were drunk" — vayyishkeru (v. 34): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render "were drunk"; PNEUMA renders "were merry." The verb shakar means "to be drunk, to drink deeply/freely." While it does not necessarily imply incapacitating intoxication, it is the standard Hebrew verb for drunkenness (the root of shikkor, "drunkard," and shekhar, "strong drink"). "Were merry" sanitizes the text. The Hebrew's plainness — they drank and were drunk with him — should be preserved. The scene's emotional release, after months of terror and guilt, is expressed in uninhibited drinking. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "were drunk."

**9. "I myself will be surety for him" — anokhi e'ervennu (v. 9): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators preserve the emphatic anokhi ("I myself") and the surety language of arav. This is the theological hinge of the chapter — Judah's personal, permanent pledge for Benjamin's safety, contrasting with Reuben's reckless offer in 42:37.

**10. "El Shaddai" preserved (v. 14): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators preserve El Shaddai untranslated, per established convention. This is the only El Shaddai invocation in the Joseph narrative proper — marking the moment Jacob releases control to divine sovereignty.

**11. "Treasure" — matmon (v. 23): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve the steward's remarkable word: "Your God and the God of your father has given you treasure in your sacks." The word matmon (from taman, "to hide, bury") — hidden treasure — attributes the returned silver to divine action.

**12. "God be gracious to you, my son" — yechonnekha Elohim beni (v. 29): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve Joseph's blessing to Benjamin — the jussive of chanan echoing the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:25, and the tender address "my son" (beni) from an elder brother to the young man he has not seen in over twenty years.

**13. "Abomination" — to'evah (v. 32): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve to'evah — the strongest term of ritual incompatibility — for the Egyptian taboo against eating with Hebrews. The irony that the narrator uses Israel's own loaded term for an Egyptian practice directed against Israelites is preserved.

**14. "Five times greater" — chamesh yadot (v. 34): All agree (3-0)**

All three agree on the five-fold ratio. The Hebrew "five hands" (chamesh yadot) is rendered as the ratio it conveys. Benjamin's conspicuous portion replicates the favoritism dynamic of 37:3 — Joseph testing whether the brothers will resent the younger son's privilege as they once resented his.

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 43–44)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 43:1 | כָּבֵד ("severe/heavy" — stative adjective) | ἐνίσχυσεν ("grew strong" — dynamic verb) | Follow MT: stative "was severe" — the famine is a settled condition, not actively intensifying |
| 43:3 | הָעֵד הֵעִד ("solemnly warned" — inf. abs. + finite) | ἐπιμαρτυρόμενος ἐμαρτύρατο ("testifying he testified") | Equivalent — both preserve the emphatic double construction |
| 43:9 | אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ ("I will be surety for him") | δέξομαι αὐτόν ("I will take him in charge") | Follow MT: surety language (arav) is specific legal terminology |
| 43:11 | מִזִּמְרַת הָאָרֶץ ("choicest produce of the land") | ἀπὸ τῶν καρπῶν τῆς γῆς ("from the fruits of the land") | Follow MT: "choicest produce" preserves the evaluative zimrah |
| 43:14 | וְאֵל שַׁדַּי ("and El Shaddai") | ὁ δὲ θεός μου ("and my God") | Follow MT: El Shaddai preserved — covenantal name lost in LXX |
| 43:14 | כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי ("as I am bereaved, I am bereaved") | εἰ ἠτέκνωμαι ἠτέκνωμαι ("if I am bereaved, I am bereaved") | Follow MT: ka'asher ("as") is not 'im ("if") — LXX adds conditional particle |
| 43:16 | טְבֹחַ טֶבַח ("slaughter a slaughter") | σφάξον θύματα ("slaughter sacrificial victims") | Follow MT: domestic slaughter, not sacrificial register |
| 43:23 | מַטְמוֹן ("treasure/hidden thing") | θησαυρούς ("treasures") | Near-equivalent — both convey stored wealth; MT's matmon emphasizes the hidden quality |
| 43:29 | יְחָנְּךָ אֱלֹהִים בְּנִי ("God be gracious to you, my son") | ὁ θεὸς ἐλεήσαι σε τέκνον ("may God have mercy on you, child") | Follow MT: chanan ("be gracious") is distinct from racham ("have mercy"); "my son" is more personal than "child" |
| 43:30 | נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו ("his compassion was stirred") | ἐταράχθη τὰ ἔγκατα αὐτοῦ ("his bowels were disturbed") | Follow MT: rachamim ("womb-love") preserves the verbal thread with v. 14; LXX relocates emotion to bowels |
| 43:32 | תּוֹעֵבָה ("abomination") | βδέλυγμα ("abomination") | Equivalent — standard LXX rendering of to'evah |
| 43:33 | וַיִּתְמְהוּ ("they marveled/were astonished") | ἐξέστησαν ("they were beside themselves") | Near-equivalent — LXX slightly stronger (ecstasy/amazement vs. wonder) |
| 43:34 | חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת ("five hands/portions") | ἑπταπλασίως ("sevenfold") | Follow MT: "five" (chamesh) against LXX "seven" — significant textual variant; all Hebrew MSS read "five" |
| 43:34 | וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ ("they were drunk / drank deeply") | ἐμεθύσθησαν ("they were made drunk") | Equivalent — both convey uninhibited drinking |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
